U4L11 - Support Vector Machines

Type date here

Front Matter

#Delete all objects from Environment - Use with caution
remove(list = ls()) 

#Load Libraries
library(tidyverse)
## ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
## ✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.6
## ✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
## ✔ ggplot2   4.0.1     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
## ✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
## ✔ purrr     1.2.1     
## ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
## ✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
## ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
## ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
library(e1071)
## 
## Attaching package: 'e1071'
## 
## The following object is masked from 'package:ggplot2':
## 
##     element
#Read in Data
heart <- read.csv("../../sample_data/L11_heart.csv")

Example - 2-dimensional Hyperplane

#Create a data frame containing the information
x1 <- seq(-1, 1, length = 10) #Create a sequence of x1 values
f <- function(x1){ #Define a function for calculating x2
  x2 = 2 - x1
}

tempDF <- data.frame(x1 = x1, x2 = f(x1))

lineplot <-
  ggplot(data = tempDF, mapping = aes(x = x1, y = x2)) + 
  geom_line(linewidth = 2)+
  labs(title = expression("2 - X"[1]*" - X"[2]*" = 0"),
       x = expression("X"[1]),
       y = expression("X"[2])) + 
  theme(axis.title = element_text(size = 25),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 20),
        title = element_text(size = 25))
lineplot

Example - 3-dimensional hyperplane

x1 <- x2 <- seq(-1, 1, length= 10)
f <- function(x1,x2){ 
  z <- x1*2 + x2 + 3 
}
x3 <- outer(x1,x2,f)
persp(x1, x2, x3, theta = 30, phi = 30, expand = 0.5, col = "lightblue",
      xlab = "X1", ylab = "X2", zlab = "X3")
title(main = expression("3 + 2X"[1]*" + X"[2]*" - X"[3]*" = 0"))

Example - Motivating Example

Completely Separated

# Set seed
set.seed(10)

# Construct sample data set - completely separated
x <- matrix(rnorm(20*2), ncol = 2)
y <- c(rep(-1,10), rep(1,10))
x[y==1,] <- x[y==1,] + 3/2
dat <- data.frame(x=x, y=as.factor(y))

# Plot data
wellsep <-
  ggplot(data = dat, aes(x = x.2, y = x.1, color = y, shape = y)) + 
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("#000000", "#FF0000")) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

wellsep

#Built SVM
# Fit Support Vector Machine model to data set
svmfit <- svm(y~., data = dat, kernel = "linear", scale = FALSE)
#While y takes on values of 1 and 2, they are character "1" and "2". So, no need to convert to a factor. If they were numeric, convert y to a factor.

# Plot Results
plot(svmfit, dat)

Not Completely Separated (builds on seed from previous example)

# Construct sample data set - not completely separated
x <- matrix(rnorm(20*2), ncol = 2)
y <- c(rep(-1,10), rep(1,10))
x[y==1,] <- x[y==1,] + 1
dat <- data.frame(x=x, y=as.factor(y))

# Plot data set
nonsep <-
  ggplot(data = dat, aes(x = x.2, y = x.1, color = y, shape = y)) + 
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("#000000", "#FF0000")) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

nonsep

# Fit Support Vector Machine model to data set
svmfit <- svm(y~., data = dat, kernel = "linear", scale = FALSE, cost = 10)
# Plot Results
plot(svmfit, dat)

Larger Dataset - Not Completely Separated (builds on seed from earlier)

# construct larger random data set
x <- matrix(rnorm(200*2), ncol = 2)
x[1:100,] <- x[1:100,] + 2.5
x[101:150,] <- x[101:150,] - 2.5
y <- c(rep(-1,150), rep(1,50))
dat <- data.frame(x=x,y=as.factor(y))

# Plot data
nonlinear <-
  ggplot(data = dat, aes(x = x.2, y = x.1, color = y, shape = y)) + 
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("#000000", "#FF0000")) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

nonlinear

# Build SVM
svmfit <- svm(y~., data = dat, kernel = "radial", gamma = 1, cost = 1)
# plot classifier
plot(svmfit, dat)

Example - Motivating SVMs Part 1

Preliminaries - Generate the dataset

#Generate Circle Data
set.seed(1432)
circle <- as.data.frame(mlbench::mlbench.circle(
  n = 200,
  d = 2
))
names(circle) <- c("x1", "x2", "y")  # rename columns

Preliminaries - Plot the datset

baseCircle <-
  ggplot(data = circle, mapping = aes(x = x1, y = x2)) +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(color = y, shape = y), size = 6, 
             alpha = 0.75, show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(x = "x1",
       y = "x2",
       title = "") + 
  xlim(-1, 1) +
  ylim(-1, 1) +
  coord_fixed() +
  theme_bw() + 
  theme(axis.title = element_text(size = 35),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 30),
        title = element_text(size = 35),
        legend.title = element_text(size = 35),
        legend.text = element_text(size = 30)) + 
  scale_color_manual(values=c("darkorange", "darkgreen"))

baseCircle

Part a - Explain why a linear decision boundary is inappropriate in this case.

No matter how you draw a linear through the points, the linear decision boundary will be a poor job of separating the classes.

Part b - Enlarge the feature space and plot

#Create new feature (x3)
circle <-
  circle %>%
  mutate(x3 = x1^2 + x2^2)
#Plot the data in 3d
rgl::plot3d(x = circle$x1, y = circle$x2, z = circle$x3, 
            col = ifelse(circle$y == "1", "darkorange", "darkgreen"),
            xlab = "x1", ylab = "x2", zlab = "x3", size = 6,
            decorate = FALSE)
#Build Support Vector Classifier in 3D Space to find the separating hyperplane
svmModel <- svm(y ~ ., data = circle, 
                kernel = "linear", cost = 10000, scale = FALSE)

#Display a summary of the model
summary(svmModel)
## 
## Call:
## svm(formula = y ~ ., data = circle, kernel = "linear", cost = 10000, 
##     scale = FALSE)
## 
## 
## Parameters:
##    SVM-Type:  C-classification 
##  SVM-Kernel:  linear 
##        cost:  10000 
## 
## Number of Support Vectors:  4
## 
##  ( 2 2 )
## 
## 
## Number of Classes:  2 
## 
## Levels: 
##  1 2
coef(svmModel)
## (Intercept)          x1          x2          x3 
##   55.009342    1.097191    1.723664  -87.590531
#Plot the 3d data and decision boundary
rgl::plot3d(x = circle$x1, y = circle$x2, z = circle$x3, 
            col = ifelse(circle$y == "1", "darkorange", "darkgreen"),
            xlab = "x1", ylab = "x2", zlab = "x3", size = 6,
            decorate = FALSE)
rgl::planes3d(a = coef(svmModel)[2], 
              b = coef(svmModel)[3], 
              c = coef(svmModel)[4], 
              d = coef(svmModel)[1], alpha = 0.5)

Example - Motivating SVMs Part 2

Build a support vector machine for classifying y using x1 and x2. Use a polynomial kernel of degree 2 and a cost 10. Do not scale the data. Then plot, the result.

#Build the model
svmModel <- svm(y~ x1 + x2, data = circle, kernel = "polynomial", degree = 2, cost = 10, scale = FALSE)
summary(svmModel)
## 
## Call:
## svm(formula = y ~ x1 + x2, data = circle, kernel = "polynomial", 
##     degree = 2, cost = 10, scale = FALSE)
## 
## 
## Parameters:
##    SVM-Type:  C-classification 
##  SVM-Kernel:  polynomial 
##        cost:  10 
##      degree:  2 
##      coef.0:  0 
## 
## Number of Support Vectors:  53
## 
##  ( 26 27 )
## 
## 
## Number of Classes:  2 
## 
## Levels: 
##  1 2
#Plot the result
plot(svmModel, data = circle, formula = x2 ~ x1)

Example - SVMs in R

Part a - Perform an 80/20 Training/Validation split using a seed of 123

#Perform a Training/Validation Split
set.seed(123)
trainInd <- sample(1:nrow(heart), floor(0.8 * nrow(heart)))
set.seed(NULL)

Train <- heart[trainInd, ]
Validation <- heart[-trainInd, ]

Part b - Train a support vector machine classifier using a radial kernel based on γ=1. Use a cost of 10 and scale the dataset.

#Build the Model
svmRadial <- svm(as.factor(HeartDisease) ~ ., data = Train, kernel = "radial", gamma = 1, cost = 10, scale = TRUE)

#Display a summary of the model
summary(svmRadial)
## 
## Call:
## svm(formula = as.factor(HeartDisease) ~ ., data = Train, kernel = "radial", 
##     gamma = 1, cost = 10, scale = TRUE)
## 
## 
## Parameters:
##    SVM-Type:  C-classification 
##  SVM-Kernel:  radial 
##        cost:  10 
## 
## Number of Support Vectors:  655
## 
##  ( 379 276 )
## 
## 
## Number of Classes:  2 
## 
## Levels: 
##  0 1

Part c - Calculate the accuracy on the validation set.

#Predict the heart disease class
predHD <- predict(svmRadial, newdata = Validation)

#Produce the confusion matrix
table(predHD, Validation$HeartDisease)
##       
## predHD  0  1
##      0 50  6
##      1 36 92
#Calculate the accuracy and error
mean(predHD ==Validation$HeartDisease)
## [1] 0.7717391
# Error rate is 1 - accuracy
1 - mean(predHD ==Validation$HeartDisease)
## [1] 0.2282609

Part d - Tune the Model. Consider combinations of cost = c(0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 5, 10,100) and gamma = c(0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4). Since we are doing cross validation, use a seed of 1. What is the optimal combination of parameters?

#Tune the model
tune.out <- tune(svm, as.factor(HeartDisease) ~.,
                 data = Train,
                 kernel = "radial", 
                 ranges = list(cost = c(0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 5, 10,100),
                               gamma = c(0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4)))

#Provide a summary
summary(tune.out)
## 
## Parameter tuning of 'svm':
## 
## - sampling method: 10-fold cross validation 
## 
## - best parameters:
##  cost gamma
##     1   0.5
## 
## - best performance: 0.1538874 
## 
## - Detailed performance results:
##     cost gamma     error dispersion
## 1  1e-03   0.5 0.4413180 0.06882926
## 2  1e-02   0.5 0.4413180 0.06882926
## 3  1e-01   0.5 0.2792484 0.07993934
## 4  1e+00   0.5 0.1538874 0.04295921
## 5  5e+00   0.5 0.1688819 0.03453020
## 6  1e+01   0.5 0.1756572 0.03998352
## 7  1e+02   0.5 0.1783969 0.04053947
## 8  1e-03   1.0 0.4413180 0.06882926
## 9  1e-02   1.0 0.4413180 0.06882926
## 10 1e-01   1.0 0.4399482 0.06933217
## 11 1e+00   1.0 0.1975194 0.05327486
## 12 5e+00   1.0 0.1947242 0.04444808
## 13 1e+01   1.0 0.1947242 0.04444808
## 14 1e+02   1.0 0.1947242 0.04444808
## 15 1e-03   2.0 0.4413180 0.06882926
## 16 1e-02   2.0 0.4413180 0.06882926
## 17 1e-01   2.0 0.4413180 0.06882926
## 18 1e+00   2.0 0.3037764 0.07112227
## 19 5e+00   2.0 0.2765087 0.06505333
## 20 1e+01   2.0 0.2765087 0.06505333
## 21 1e+02   2.0 0.2765087 0.06505333
## 22 1e-03   3.0 0.4413180 0.06882926
## 23 1e-02   3.0 0.4413180 0.06882926
## 24 1e-01   3.0 0.4413180 0.06882926
## 25 1e+00   3.0 0.3378378 0.05838903
## 26 5e+00   3.0 0.3269160 0.05938799
## 27 1e+01   3.0 0.3269160 0.05938799
## 28 1e+02   3.0 0.3269160 0.05938799
## 29 1e-03   4.0 0.4413180 0.06882926
## 30 1e-02   4.0 0.4413180 0.06882926
## 31 1e-01   4.0 0.4413180 0.06882926
## 32 1e+00   4.0 0.3691225 0.05989749
## 33 5e+00   4.0 0.3459830 0.06525063
## 34 1e+01   4.0 0.3459830 0.06525063
## 35 1e+02   4.0 0.3459830 0.06525063
#Find the optimal combination of cost and gamma
tune.out$best.parameters
##   cost gamma
## 4    1   0.5

Part e - Based on the optimal values of the tuning parameters, produce the confusion matrix and accuracy for the validation data.

#Obtain predictions using the optimal combination of cost and gamma
predHD <- predict(tune.out$best.model, newdata = Validation)

#Create the confusion matrix
table(predHD, Validation$HeartDisease)
##       
## predHD  0  1
##      0 59  9
##      1 27 89
#Calculate the accuracy and error rate
mean(Validation$HeartDisease == predHD)
## [1] 0.8043478
1 - mean(Validation$HeartDisease == predHD)
## [1] 0.1956522

Part f - How does the performance of the SVM classifier compare a logistic regression containing all of the features? (Use a threshold of 0.5.)

#Build the logistic regression model
glmModel <- glm(as.factor(HeartDisease) ~., 
                data = Train,
                family = "binomial")
  
#Obtain the predicted probabilities and predicted classes
predProb <- predict(glmModel, newdata = Validation, type = "response")
threshold <- 0.5
predClass <- ifelse(predProb > threshold, 1, 0)

#Create the confusion matrix
table(predClass, Validation$HeartDisease)

#Calculate the accuracy and error rate
mean(predClass == Validation$HeartDisease)
1 - mean(predClass == Validation$HeartDisease)

Part g - What does the result in the previous part suggest about the decision boundary?

The relationship is linear.
